title-group.article-title: Osteometric and Morphologic Study of Appendicular Skeletons of Female Simien Sheep in Wogera District, Northwest Ethiopia

aff.institution: College of Agriculture and Environmental Sciences, Bahir Dar University
College of Veterinary Medicine and Animal Science, University of Gondar

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Simien sheep are the predominant fat‐tailed sheep breeds found in Northwest Ethiopia, playing a significant role in the livelihood of resource‐poor farmers. This study provides the morphometric and morphological descriptions of the appendicular skeletons of Ethiopia's indigenous female Simien sheep. The reason for this work was the dearth of knowledge regarding the osteometry and morphology of appendicular skeletons in domestic animals, particularly the indigenous sheep of Ethiopia. Hence, it has been done to generate osteometrical data and investigate morphological features. Two age groups ([12–18 months] and [19–24 months]) of 10 female Simien sheep were purchased from Wogera district. Sheep with a lack of observable skeletal abnormalities, non‐pregnant and lactating and in good health were purposively selected. The chemical method standard procedures were adopted for specimen preparation. Osteometrical measurements and morphological investigations were taken and summarized using descriptive statistics (mean ± SD) and independent sample t‐tests. The measurement values were significantly (p < 0.05) increased with age across groups in most of the variables; however, a significant difference was not observed in scapula (breadth of the glenoid cavity), humerus, ulna, metacarpal, femur and tibia (smallest breadth of the diaphysis), metacarpal and femur (smallest circumference of the diaphysis), metatarsal measurement parameters and weight of metatarsals. The study demonstrated that the anatomy of the appendicular skeletons of Simien sheep differed slightly, both morphologically and morphometrically, from other ruminants. The findings on the osteometric and morphologic characteristics can be used as a contribution to a comparative model for other ovine breeds in general and Ethiopian sheep breeds in particular. It is therefore recommended that further work and studies should be carried out on other age groups, ecotypes and other indigenous sheep breeds, and investigation on the effect of age and sexual dimorphism should be important. It is imagined that the results obtained will be useful as baseline research data for further surgical and comparative anatomical studies.

fn.p: Funding: The authors received no specific funding for this work.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The Eastern African sheep are classified as either fat‐tailed or fat‐rumped (Muigai and Hanotte 2013). Sheep are known for their versatility and adaptability, which allow for their presence on every continent and landscape, serving different functions and reaching a population of more than 1.2 billion heads worldwide (Canton 2021). With approximately 40 million heads, Ethiopia has the second‐largest sheep population in Africa (CSA [Ethiopia] and ICF International 2012). The country's diversified ecology, which ranges from sub‐alpine to lowlands, as well as its ethnic communities and production systems, are inhabited by a diverse range of sheep breeds (Tibbo 2006; Gizaw et al. 2013, 2007; Ayele and Urge 2018). Fourteen traditional sheep populations are clustered into four major groups of sheep types based on their tail type, namely, short fat‐tailed, long fat‐tailed, thin‐tailed and fat‐rumped tailed sheep (Galall 1983), and nine breeds and six genetically distinct sheep breed groups in the country (Gizaw et al. 2007). The northwest highlands are among the major sheep production areas of the country, where the Simien sheep breed and their crosses with other indigenous sheep are widely distributed (Gizaw et al. 2008; Deribe et al. 2021).

The skeletal system is a fundamental structure for distinguishing animal species, particularly in veterinary education, where detailed anatomical knowledge underpins diagnostic and surgical skills (Peter and Rogers 1980). With advancements in artificial intelligence (AI), analysing anatomical data has become increasingly efficient and precise, especially in veterinary anatomy (Choudhary et al. 2024). Recently, AI and machine learning have opened new avenues for analysing anatomical data, offering enhanced precision and efficiency in veterinary diagnostics and surgery (Vickram et al. 2024). These tools not only improve diagnostic accuracy but also enable sophisticated surgical planning and personalized veterinary healthcare, underscoring the growing importance of AI in modern veterinary medicine. Furthermore, AI‐powered tools enhance diagnostic capabilities and provide new opportunities for teaching and research through interactive and dynamic tools such as 3D modelling and augmented reality (Choudhary et al. 2024). These developments highlight the increasing integration of AI in veterinary medicine, transforming traditional practices into more data‐driven, efficient methodologies.

The evaluation of sheep bones provides morphological data, which can be used to define the animal population and allows comparison with other historic or modern sheep populations (Davis 1996; Guintard and Lallemand 2003; Alpak et al. 2009; Davis 2000). Several authors (Onar et al. 2008; Alpak et al. 2009; Salami et al. 2011; Pazvant et al. 2015; Demiraslan et al. 2015, 2018; Azarpajouh et al. 2021; Blagojević et al. 2016) have reported the normal anatomy of the appendicular skeletons in sheep. Extensive research also has documented the anatomy of the appendicular skeleton in sheep, creating a valuable reference for diagnosing musculoskeletal disorders and injuries, comparing closely related species and serving as animal models (Schummer and Seiferle 1977; Salami et al. 2011; Lochi et al. 2014; Zedda et al. 2017; Salvagno and Albarella 2017). Recent advances in veterinary anatomy education highlight the integration of digital platforms like ChatGPT, which offer interactive and accessible support for learning complex anatomical structures, enhancing comprehension for students and professionals alike (Choudhary et al. 2023). Tools like ChatGPT allow for flexible learning opportunities in veterinary anatomy, aiding researchers, educators and students by providing detailed information on bone morphology and physiology. Such resources can supplement practical experience, offering new perspectives on animal anatomy's role in understanding evolutionary biology, forensic science and surgical practices.

To understand the biology and pathophysiology of organisms, many biomedical disciplines heavily rely on quantitative data from animal bones. Sheep are being used more frequently for teaching, training and biomedical research (Potes et al. 2008; Isaenkov et al. 2019). In addition, it is commonly used as a model for the evaluation of implants in the treatment of osteochondral defects and fracture management (Potes et al. 2008). The osteological examination is also very important in terms of the identification and study of animal evolution (Zeder and Lapham 2010). In appendicular skeletons, it reveals differences within and between species, developmental changes and defining a certain population in varying ecology and management conditions (Alpak et al. 2009; Salami et al. 2011; Salvagno and Albarella 2017). Limb bones are frequently used to assess the size and morphological changes that occur during domestication (Onar et al. 2008), and they may help to better understand the adaptive changes that the focal species have undergone throughout their evolution from a common ancestor (Zedda et al. 2017). Researching sheep's skeletal osteometry and morphology is crucial in several scientific disciplines, including forensic medicine, surgery, evolutionary biology and comparative anatomy (Payne 2017; Teichert 1975; Pourlis et al. 2014).

Despite numerous studies on the osteometry and morphology of limb bones across various sheep breeds globally (Davis 1996; Guintard and Lallemand 2003; Alpak et al. 2009; Isaenkov et al. 2019; Azarpajouh et al. 2021) and other species (Olawoye et al. 2011; Abdullah‐Al‐Mahmud and Mussa 2016; Gündemir et al. 2020; Sathyamoorthy et al. 2020; Abdulrahman and Abubakar Sadiq 2021; Jahan 2020), there is a notable gap in the data for Ethiopia's indigenous sheep breeds, especially the Simien breed. This study, therefore, aims to address this gap by generating osteometric data and investigating the gross morphological characteristics of the pectoral and pelvic limb bones of female Simien sheep from the Wogera district in the Central Gondar Zone. The findings will contribute valuable baseline data for comparative anatomy and support veterinary education, enabling a deeper understanding of the unique anatomical features of this breed and holding relevance to AI in its potential application of machine learning models to analyse morphometric datasets for identifying skeletal abnormalities, enhancing surgical precision and facilitating predictive diagnostics and supporting AI‐driven systems in veterinary medicine by building robust databases for automated morphometric assessments and fostering advancements in diagnostics, teaching and research.

sec.title: Study Area
sec.p: The study was carried out on an indigenous female Simien sheep breed from Wogera district, located at an average altitude of 2812 m above sea level. It is found between 37.30° E and 13.00° N longitude (Figure 1) and at an altitude in the northern highlands of Ethiopia. It is located 763 km north of the capital of the country, Addis Ababa and 36 km from its zonal capital city of Gondar. The rainfall pattern is bimodal, with a short rainy season from March to May, followed by a long rainy season from June to September. It has an average annual rainfall of 700 mm, and the temperature varies from 10°C to 28°C with a mean annual temperature of 12.7°C (NMA 2008). Livestock is the major agricultural resource in the area and has a population of 209,733 cattle, 288,448 sheep, 88,761 goats, 38,278 equines and 309,465 poultry (WWADO 2022). The district was chosen because, besides being the highest sheep‐producing district in the region, it has a higher population of Simien sheep compared to other districts. Wogera district is largely rural with subsistence farming and animal husbandry being the major activities. Animal husbandry is dominated by sheep rearing, mainly reared on free‐range management due to prevailing adverse climatic and environmental conditions (Melaku et al. 2019).

caption.p: Map of Ethiopia showing the study area in Wogera, Northwest Ethiopia.

sec.title: Study Animals
sec.p: The study was conducted on 10 female Simien sheep, specifically selected from the Wogera district. The sample size was determined on the basis of the availability of healthy, non‐pregnant and non‐lactating individuals with no skeletal deformities or lameness from Wogera district flocks, adhering to ethical guidelines for animal welfare (WOAH 2021). The study selected healthy, non‐pregnant, non‐lactating females with animals who were transported to the University of Gondar Veterinary Anatomy Laboratory for examination.

The selected age groups (12–18 and 19–24 months) focus on post‐pubertal development, allowing for analysis of bone growth maturity in the appendicular skeleton. The first age group captures rapid growth post‐puberty, while the second represents a phase nearing skeletal maturity, revealing transitions in osteometric and morphologic characteristics. These ranges are commonly used in research to standardize comparisons across post‐pubertal studies, enabling alignment with other findings.

sec.title: Study Design
sec.p: Exploratory experimental study design and purposive sampling were used in this study. The age was estimated on the basis of dentition as guided by Vatta et al. (2006).

sec.title: Preparation of the Specimen
sec.p: The chemical preparation technique was used to prepare the skeletal specimens. The animals were skinned and dissected by removing the muscles, ligaments and tendons of the whole body except for some small fragments of muscles and tendons on the bones. As much of the soft tissue as possible was then removed before maceration to accelerate skeletal preparation (Saulsman et al. 2010). Then the bones were boiled with 3% NaOH for about 2 h. After cooling, the additional muscles and tissues were removed from the bones. Then after, the bones were allowed to dry for 24 h. The small tissue fragments on the bone and fat were removed by putting them in ammonia (NH3) (100 mL/gallon of water) for 7 days to get clean bones completely (Hussain et al. 2007). The bones were dried at a natural temperature for 24 h. After that, they were whitened by dipping in 4% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution for 48 h and dried (Hussain et al. 2007). After recovery, the scapula, humerus, radius, ulna, metacarpal, os coxae, femur, tibia and metatarsal were selected for further osteometric analysis and morphological investigation.

sec.title: Osteometric Analysis
sec.p: Morphometrical measurements were obtained on pectoral and pelvic limb bones using an osteometric board, digital calliper (sensitivity of 0.01 mm), measuring tape and electronic precision balance (Model: CTG1200‐1200, a sensitivity of 0.01 g). Some measurements were not performed if the bone was damaged to avoid biasing the results. Various distinguished gross anatomical features and measured values were recorded on the left and right limb bones. The measurements were performed according to the Davis (1996) and Von Den Driesch (1976) standards. At the same time, it was systematically recorded according to parameters corresponding to the used standards. Descriptions of the measurement parameters by Von Den Driesch (1976) in the form of a sketch (Davis 1996) as a descriptive essay were available, and the measurements were defined by Boessneck (1971) and Teichert (1975). As Von Den Driesch (1976) pointed out, all measurements must be performed by the chief researcher to minimize measurement errors. Definitions and measurement techniques were clarified before the study.

sec.title: Osteo‐Morphological Investigation
sec.p: After the skeletons were prepared and dried, the selected bones were anatomically positioned to notify their morphology. The form (shape, size and position) and featuring structures of the pectoral and pelvic limb bones of the female Simien sheep breed were studied and compared using the information of May (1970) as a guide. The naming of bones and featuring structures also derived from Nomina Anatomica Veterinaria (2017) by translating the official Latin terms into the language of instruction. Photographs of bone specimens were obtained using a digital camera (TECNO Camon CX Air; HiOS v2.2.0, 1280 × 720 screen resolutions). The pictures were taken from fully fused bones to take part in the morphological characteristics.

sec.title: Data Analysis
sec.p: Data analysis in the current study was done on osteometric and morphologic examinations of the appendicular skeletons. The data was entered and stored in an Excel spreadsheet and later imported into the software for analysis.

Osteometrical analysis was undertaken for each element individually, using age as the grouping variable, selected bones as an independent variable and the chosen measurement parameters as the dependent variables. Then after, the recorded osteometric measurements were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (SD), coefficient of variation (CV) for each measurement obtained for a general description of the limbs and further statistically analysed by using Statistical Package for Social Sciences (IBM, SPSS 22.0) packaged software. An independent sample t‐test to compare the groups and a significance level of p ≤ 0.05 were used.

Descriptive statistics were used to explain the morphological findings. Thus, there was no inferential statistical treatment done on the observations made on the female Simien sheep appendicular skeleton which were recorded and compared using descriptive terms based on the information of May (1970).

sec.title: Results
sec.p: This study has documented the osteometry and morphology of the appendicular skeletons of the female Simien sheep breed. The results obtained from the baseline data for the sheep's scapula, humerus, radius, ulna, metacarpal, os coxae, femur, tibia and metatarsal. The osteometric data generation and morphological characteristics are reported below.

sec.title: Osteometric Measurements
sec.p: As shown in Table 1, the tibia was the heaviest bone, whereas the metatarsal was the lightest and had low variability in measurements among others. The magnitude of the differences within a set of measurements (SD) was recorded in the weight of the selected bones of the two age groups. A statistically significant difference (p < 0.05) was observed between the groups except on metatarsal bones.

caption.p: Comparison between the two age groups of female Simien sheep based on weight (g) of appendicular skeletons.

fn.p: Note: N, total number of samples taken.

sec.p: The mean, SD and CV of the female Simien sheep morphometric values of the appendicular skeletons were determined and are shown in Tables 2 and 3, with the animals subdivided into two age groups (12–18 and 19–24 months). As certain measurements could not be performed, the number of samples for each measurement differs. The reason was a fragment of bone with a missing part. Therefore, the frequencies of measurement variables have been varied.

caption.p: Osteometric measurements of forelimb bones in younger and older ages of female Simien sheep breed.

fn.p: Note: Number of individuals examined (N), mean value of each measurement (μ), minimum–maximum range (Min–Max). All values in mm, CV in %. p < 0.05.

Abbreviations: BT, breadth trochlea; CV, coefficient of variation; DHA, diagonal height along; GL, general length; GLP, greatest length of articular process; HS, height along the spine; SD, standard deviation.

caption.p: Osteometric measurements of hindlimb bones in younger and older ages of female Simien sheep breed.

fn.p: Note: Number of individuals examined (N), mean value of each measurement (μ), minimum–maximum range (Min–Max). All values in mm, CV in %. p < 0.05.
Abbreviations: CV, coefficient of variation; GL, general length; SB, smallest breadth; SD, standard deviation; SH, smallest height.

sec.p: An independent sample t‐test was conducted to compare the measurement variables for two age groups. Most of the measurement variables were significantly different. But, the insignificant differences in measurement values of scapula (length of the glenoid cavity, breadth of the glenoid cavity), humerus (smallest breadth [SB] of the diaphysis), radius (SB of the diaphysis), metacarpal (greatest breadth of the distal end, SB of the diaphysis and smallest circumference of the diaphysis), os coxae (SB of the shaft of ilium, greatest breadth across the acetabulum), femur (SB of the diaphysis, smallest circumference of the diaphysis), tibia (greatest breadth of the distal end, SB of the diaphysis) and metatarsal (general length [GL], greatest breadth of the distal end, SB of the diaphysis, smallest circumference of the diaphysis) were shown.

sec.title: Osteo‐Morphological Investigations
sec.p: This part of the study investigated the morphology of the female Simien sheep appendicular skeletons by examination of the different surfaces of the scapula, humerus, radius, ulna, metacarpal, os coxae, femur, tibia and metatarsal bones based on the position of featuring structures present. They were grossly examined and labelled the observed features individually.

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of the Scapula
sec.p: The scapula was flat and triangular, relatively wider at the dorsal end and narrower at the ventral end (Figure 2A). The scapula presented two surfaces and three borders. The medial surface presented deeper sub‐scapular fossa (Figure 2A,B). A prominent scapular spine divided the lateral surface into a smaller supra‐spinous and a much larger infra‐spinous fossa (Figure 2A). The vertebral border of the scapula was straight. The scapular spine was extended up to the neck in an almost straight line and the free edge was prominent. The free edge of the spine was not subsided by the acromion process and was less distinct. The neck of the scapula was very short and demarcated (Figure 2B). The nutrient foramen in the scapula of Simien sheep was found at the neck of the scapula. The central tuberosity of the spine was less distinct (Figure 2A). The angle created by the acromion process was almost a straight line in Simien sheep. The articular surface of the glenoid cavity was rounded and concaves (Figure 2B). The coracoid process was ill‐distinct in Simien sheep.

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the scapula of Simien sheep. (A) Right scapula bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) coracoid process, (2) acromion process, (3) scapular spine, (4) supraspinatus fossa, (5) infraspinatus fossa, (6) dorsal/vertebral border, (7) caudal border, (8) cranial border, (9) scapular spine tuber, (10) cranial angle and (11) caudal angle; (B) right scapula bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) supraglenoid tubercle, (2) neck, (3) subscapular fossa, (4) glenoid cavity and (5) scapular spine.

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of Humerus
sec.p: When the entire bone was investigated, it was evident that there was a slight curvature in both the mediolateral and craniocaudal views (Figure 3A–C). The proximal extremity of the humerus has two tubercules and a medially projecting head (medial and lateral). In Simien sheep, the minor tubercule was considerably less than the lateral tuberosity. The cranial part of the tuberosities’ blunt end showed slight incurving (Figure 3A–C). Medial tuberosity was less prominent (Figure 3A). The medial and lateral tuberosities were separated by the bicipital groove. Both the medial and lateral tuberosities’ anterior portions are bent over the bicipital groove (Figure 3A). An elliptical shape can be observed in the head as it reaches the lateral tuberosity. The shaft was cylindrical and slightly twisted. The lateral surface had a musculospiral groove and was spiral‐shaped and smooth (Figure 3B). The deltoid tuberosity was found less prominent. The nutrient foramen was noticed more proximally located near the middle of the caudal surface.

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the humerus of Simien sheep. (A) Right humerus bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) head, (2) bicipital groove, (3) major tubercule, (4) olecranon fossa, (5) medial epicondyle, (6) lateral epicondyle and (7) neck; (B) left humerus bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) major tubercule, (2) bicipital groove, (3) minor tubercule, (4) musculospiral groove, (5) trochlea, (6) capitulum and (7) radial fossa with nutrient foramen; (C) left humerus bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) anterior part of the major tubercule, (2) posterior part of the major tubercule, (3) head, (4) radial fossa, (5) medial condyle and (6) lateral condyle; (D) left humerus bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) major tubercule, (2) minor tubercule, (3) head, (4) neck, (5) medial surface, (6) medial condyle and (7) articular surface.

sec.p: Two condyles, indistinct epicondyles and radial and olecranon fossae made up the distal extremity. The lateral condyle was considerably smaller than the medial condyle (Figure 3A,C,D). A significant fossa was observed on the lateral aspect of the lateral condyle but not across the medial condyle. The olecranon fossa was deeper and the radial fossa was shallower (Figure 3A,B). A shallow olecranon fossa was observed on the cranial surface of the humerus’ condyle. Additionally, a concave radial fossa extended mediolaterally and sloped from proximomedial to distolateral (Figure 3D). The articular surface of the humerus’ condyle displayed a laterally located capitulum and a medially situated, grooved trochlea (Figure 3B). The trochlea extended more proximally than the capitulum and was larger than it (Figure 3B).

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of Radius and Ulna
sec.p: The radius was faintly curved and presented a shaft and two ends. The long shaft was flattened craniocaudally and curved longitudinally. The caudal surface was concave, and throughout the whole length along the lateral part, it was attached to the cranial surface of the shaft of the ulna except at the two interosseous spaces (Figure 4A,B). The body of the radius had a convex cranial surface and a concave caudal surface (Figure 4A). It was larger and stout than the ulna. In the middle of the upper part of the anterior surface, there was a rough elevation, known as radial tuberosity; it was represented by a rough area just distal to the head of the radius on the craniomedial surface of the body (Figure 4A). The medial styloid process of the radius was wide and convex (Figure 4B).

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the radius and ulna of Simien sheep. (A) Right radius and ulna bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) olecranon tuber, (2) anconeal process, (3) trochlear notch, (4) radial tubercule, (5) proximal interosseous space, (6) shaft of radius and (7) lateral styloid process; (B) right radius and ulna bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) radial neck, (2) radial head, (3) lateral coronoid process, (4) body of the ulna, (5) medial styloid process, (6) olecranon process of ulna, (7) radial articular facets, (8) lateral styloid process and (9) distal interosseous space.

sec.p: The thin curved shaft and the distal extremity of the ulna were fused to a limited narrow area on the caudal surface of the radius. The two bones were presented with two interosseous spaces (Figure 4A,B). The distal interosseous space was small and indistinct (Figure 4B). The proximal end was expanded and comprised one large olecranon process and a trochlear notch (Figure 4A) and articulates with the condyles of the humerus, whereas the distal extremity was placed laterally to the radius (Figure 4B). At the anterior border of the trochlear notch, there was a beak‐like projection, and the anconeal process formed (Figure 4A). The caudally directed olecranon process of the ulna was elongated and projected proximally beyond the radius (Figure 4A). The olecranon tuber was elliptical (Figure 4A). The cranial rim of the proximal articular surface presents an ill‐distinct projection, known as a coronoid process (Figure 4B). The distal end is articulated with the proximal row of carpal bones and presents three articular surfaces (Figure 4B). The lateral styloid process of the ulna at the distal extremity was convex pointed and extended below the radius (Figure 4A,B).

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of Metacarpus
sec.p: In Simien sheep, the metacarpus consisted of a fused third and fourth metacarpal bone, which is only the large metacarpal, as observed (Figure 5A,B). The dorsal and palmar surfaces had a dorsal and palmar longitudinal groove, respectively, near the midline (Figure 5A,B). In the proximal and distal fourth of the grooves, the body presented a metacarpal canal known as nutrient foramen (Figure 5A,B). The distal palmar nutrient foramen was more distal than the dorsal (Figure 5B). Medial to the dorsal longitudinal groove, the dorsal surface had a metacarpal tuberosity (Figure 5A). The articular surface of the base of the fused third and fourth metacarpal bones was divided into two parts by a sagittal ridge (Figure 5A). The body of the fused third and fourth metacarpal was convex and had flattened dorsal and palmar surfaces (Figure 5A,B), respectively. The two concave articular areas of the proximal extremity were presented in Simien sheep.

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the metacarpal of Simien sheep. (A) Right metacarpal bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) metacarpal tuberosity, (2) dorsal longitudinal groove, (3) distal nutrient foramen, (4) sagittal ridge, (5) inter‐condyloid cleft, (6) lateral condyle, (7) medial condyle and (8) shaft; (B) metacarpal bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) palmar longitudinal groove, (2) facets for sesamoid bone, (3) distal palmar nutrient foramen, (4) medial condyle and (5) lateral condyle.

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of Os Coxae
sec.p: The pelvic girdle (os coxae) was a flat, irregular bone in Simien sheep (Figure 6A,B). It consisted of two halves which were joined at the pelvic symphysis in the midline. Each part in turn consisted of three parts: the ilium, the ischium and the pubis. Ilium was the largest of the three divisions and was irregularly triangular. The bone was flat and expanded above, narrow in the middle and slightly expanded below. The cranial border of the ilium bone was thick (known as an iliac crest), rough at the cranial ends and thin at the caudal end (Figure 6A,B). The wing of the ilium formed the sacral and coxal tubers at its medial and lateral angles, respectively (Figure 6B). The ischium was placed behind the ilium and the pubis. The cranial border forms the caudal margin of the obturator foramen (Figure 6A,B). Its craniolateral angle formed the caudal part of the acetabulum, and its caudolateral angle formed the ischial tuber, whereas the caudomedial aspect joined its opposite to form an ischiatic arch (Figure 6A). The pubis was the smallest bone of the os coxae and was placed between the ilium and ischium (Figure 6A,B). The medial border of the ischium meets with the opposite part and forms the ischial symphysis (Figure 6A). The medial border of the pubis is joined with the same border of the opposite bone at the pubic symphysis (Figure 6A). The posterior border of the pubis was concave and formed the anterior boundary of the obturator foramen. Both the ischial symphysis and pubic symphysis were not ossified. The acetabulum was formed by the union of ilium, ischium and pubis (Figure 6A). On the ventral surface of the body cranial to the acetabulum, supra‐acetabular fossa was presented (Figure 6A). The obturator foramen (Figure 6A,B) was a large oval opening found on the floor of the pelvis and was formed by the ischium and the pubis.

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on os coxae of Simien sheep. (A) Os coxae bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) pubis, (2) ischium, (3) ilium, (4) obturator foramen, (5) acetabulum, (6) coxal tuber, (7) ischiatic tuberosity, (8) pubic symphysis, (9) ischiatic notch, (10) iliac crest, (11) ischiatic arch, (12) sacral tuber, (13) supra acetabular fossa and (14) ischial symphysis; (B) os coxae bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) ilium, (2) wing of ilium, (3) iliac crest, (4) sacral tuber, (5) pubic symphysis, (6) ischiatic tuber, (7) obturator foramen, (8) pubis, (9) ischium and (10) coxal tuber.

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of Femur
sec.p: Different morphological details were observed in the Simien sheep femur, especially about the proximal and distal epiphyses and the shaft. Observing the whole bone, it appears straighter, where a slight curvature in cranial, caudo‐medial and caudal views was appreciable (Figure 7A,B). It was the second largest and the most massive long bone next to the tibia in Simien sheep. The proximal extremity of the femur consisted of the head and neck medially and the greater trochanter laterally. The head showed an elliptical shape gradually passing into the trochanteric fossa (Figure 7A,B). The greater trochanter was placed laterally and was very massive; its lateral surface was convex and rough, and the medial surface was concave (Figure 7A,B). The shaft was nearly cylindrical. A small nutrient foramen was present in the proximal part of the cranial surface. The distal extremity was large and composed of a trochlea in front and two condyles behind (Figure 7C). The trochlea was composed of two ridges of lips separated by a groove. The medial and lateral ridges did not have a prominent size difference (Figure 7A). The condyles were medial and lateral; they were separated by a deep inter‐condyloid fossa (Figure 7C). The lateral femoral condyle was larger than the medial femoral condyle. The supracondyloid fossa was visible on the distal part of the femur. The curved trochanteric crest connected the greater trochanter obliquely to the lesser trochanter while creating the trochanteric fossa (Figure 7A). The distal extremity presented caudally; the lateral condyle and the obliquely directed medial condyle are separated by the intercondylar fossa. Cranially, it presented a trochlea with two unequal ridges (Figure 7B).

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the femur of Simien sheep. (A) Right femur bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) head, (2) trochanteric fossa, (3) greater trochanter, (4) neck, (5) lesser trochanter, (6) medial condyle, (7) inter‐condyloid fossa, (8) lateral condyle, (9) intertrochanteric crest and (10) supracondyloid fossa; (B) right femur bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) greater trochanter, (2) head, (3) neck, (4) shaft and (5) trochlea; (C) right femur bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) head, (2) greater trochanter, (3) neck, (4) medial surface, (5) medial condyle, (6) lateral condyle, (7) trochlea and (8) intercondylar fossa.

sec.title: Morphological Examination Tibia
sec.p: It presented a shaft and two extremities (proximal and distal). The shaft is slightly cylindrical, except near the extremities, where it is wider and compressed backwards (Figure 8A,B). The proximal extremity consisted of the lateral and medial condyles separated by an intertuberal fossa, two articular eminences (facets for lateral and medial condyles), tibial spine and tibial tuberosities (Figure 8A–C). The tibial tuberosities were separated by intertuberal fossa (Figure 8B). The tibial spine (Figure 8B) was a central articular eminence divided into medial and lateral parts. The distal extremity was much smaller than the proximal (Figure 8A,B). There was presented an articular surface marked with depressions for articulation with the tibia tarsal bone (Figure 8C). The fibula was absent, whereas the lateral malleolus was formed by a small irregular malleoli bone.

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the tibia of Simien sheep. (A) Left tibia bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) tibial crest, (2) lateral condyles, (3) medial condyle, (4) tibial tubercule, (5) muscular sulcus, (6) malleolar bone and (7) cranial surface; (B) right tibia bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1 and 2) facets for condyles, (3) smaller tibial tubercules, (4) tibial crest, (5) malleolar bone, (6) medial malleolus and (7) inter tuberal fossa; (C) tibia bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) medial condyle, (2) lateral condyle, (3) shaft, (4) medial malleolus, (5) malleolar bone, (6) facets for the articulation of tarsals and (7) popliteal line.

sec.title: Morphological Examinations of Metatarsus
sec.p: The metatarsal bone had the shaft, proximal and distal extremities (Figure 9). The proximal extremity presented articular surfaces for tarsal articulation, and its dorsal surface was smooth. At the proximal end, it presents a metatarsal tuberosity, a small concave facet behind the metatarsal tuberosity and lateral articular areas. The distal extremity comprised lateral and medial condyle separated by an intercondyloid cleft, facets for sesamoid bone and axial articular area. The divided condyles were not straight. Each condyle presented a median ridge. A shallow longitudinal groove was found at the proximal one‐third of the dorsal surface. The metatarsal canal was seen on the proximal and distal parts (Figure 9).

caption.p: Photograph of morphological features on the metatarsal bone of Simien sheep. Metatarsal bone of yearling Simien sheep showing (1) proximal extremity, (2) plantar longitudinal groove, (3) dorsal longitudinal groove, (4) facets for sesamoid bone, (5) anterior groove, (6 and 7) distal extremity having condyles for articulation with phalanx, (8) inter‐condyloid cleft, (9) lateral condyle, (10) medial condyle, (11) axial articular area and (12) distal metatarsal canal.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: To the best of our knowledge and according to the available literature, this is going to be the first study in Ethiopia that has documented the osteometric and morphologic characteristics of female Simien sheep's scapula, humerus, radius, ulna, metacarpal, os coxae, femur, tibia and metatarsal. Due to the lack of data on these limb bones in Ethiopian sheep breeds and other animals, discussions were made from various other literature sources.

sec.title: Osteometric Measurements
sec.p: Bone weight and length growth depend primarily on the electrolyte concentration, especially calcium and phosphorus (Richardson et al. 1976; Church 1991). These electrolytes are not synthesized by the body and are therefore dependent on the mineral content of the feed, feed intake and mineral availability (Church 1991; Sivachelvan et al. 1996). It is, therefore, pertinent to point out that the nutritional status of animals used in this research from which the bone specimens were prepared was unknown. Several factors have been reported to influence the growth and development of bone tissue. Intrinsic (genetic and hormonal) and extrinsic (environmental and dietary) factors also interact to influence bone development and growth (Lawrence et al. 2012). Among these factors, intrinsic factors are relevant in this study. As the study animals came from the same environment, bone weight differences may be related to differences in feed conversion efficiency, history of health problems and differences in management conditions between farmers.

The height along the spine (HS), diagonal height along the spine (DHA) and greatest length of articular process (GLP) of scapula in Simien female sheep were 127.71 ± 5.47, 143.75 ± 5.97 and 28.16 ± 0.93 mm and 148.38 ± 8.96, 160.75 ± 6.98 and 30.84 ± 2.27 mm (Table 2) in younger and older age groups, respectively, whereas in adult female Morkaraman sheep, they were 171.49 ± 5.984, 179.38 ± 6.091 and 38.08 ± 1.483, respectively, on the same measurement parameters (Alpak et al. 2009); however, Demiraslan et al. (2018) reported GLP (33.8 ± 3.2) in Kibyra sheep. The SB of the shaft of ilium and the smallest shaft of the height of ilium (SH) of os coxae in Simien sheep were 12.61 ± 0.85 and 14.80 ± 1.81 mm in younger ages and 13.93 ± 0.89 and 16.30 ± 1.06 mm in older age groups, respectively; however, in Kibrya sheep they were 8.2 ± 2.0 and 13.1 ± 1.1 mm, respectively.

The GL of the humerus of the female Simien sheep was 133.90 ± 4.04 mm (Table 2). However, the average greatest length in adult Morkaraman sheep was 161.18 ± 4.017 mm (Alpak et al. 2009), and the average greatest length of the humerus of chital was 19.86 ± 0.04 cm (Choudhary et al. 2013). The average breadth trochlea (BT) and greatest breadth of the distal end (Bd) in female Simien sheep were 18.16 ± 1.34 and 25.81 ± 1.18 mm and 20.24 ± 0.66 and 27.81 ± 1.37 mm in younger and older ages, respectively (Table 2), whereas the same parameter was found to be 27.2 ± 2.2 and 29.8 ± 2.2 mm in Kibyra sheep, respectively (Demiraslan et al. 2018). The greatest length, breadth of the proximal end, breadth of the distal end and SB of the diaphysis of the femur were 162.60 ± 2.46, 36.74 ± 1.44, 32.14 ± 1.31 and 12.95 ± 0.30 mm in younger groups and 179.40 ± 10.96, 39.99 ± 1.77, 35.14 ± 1.84 and 13.72 ± 1.23 mm in older groups, respectively; however, the measurement for adult Morkaraman sheep by Alpak et al. (2009) was 188.15 ± 4.720, 50.09 ± 2.039, 43.16 ± 1.305 and 18.88 ± 0.933 mm, respectively, whereas the average length of the femur of blackbuck was 169.70 ± 02.0 mm in Indian blackbuck (Choudhary and Ishwer Singh 2016). The greatest breadth of the proximal end and distal end for Kibrya sheep by Demiraslan et al. (2018) was 39.9 and 37.0 ± 1.2 mm, respectively.

The average greatest breadth of the proximal end, distal end and smallest circumference of the diaphysis in the radius of female Simien sheep was 26.86 ± 1.39, 25.36 ± 1.11 and 13.13 ± 0.66 mm in younger age groups and 29.23 ± 1.46, 26.58 ± 1.24 and 14.32 ± 0.67 mm in older age groups, respectively. However, 29.7 ± 3.6, 28.7 ± 3.0 and 15.9 ± 1.7 mm in Kibrya sheep (Demiraslan et al. 2018) and 35.93 ± 1.103, 33.48 ± 0.985 and 17.68 ± 0.748 mm in Morkaraman sheep (Alpak et al. 2009), respectively. The smallest depth of the olecranon of the ulna in female Simien sheep was 19.1 ± 1.54 and 32.96 ± 1.61 in younger groups and 20.78 ± 1.63 and 37.84 ± 1.99 in older age groups, respectively, whereas the measurement for Kibrya sheep by Demiraslan et al. (2018) was 21.9 ± 2.1 and 38.2 ± 5.2 mm, respectively. The greatest length and depth of the anconeal process for adult Morkaraman sheep by Alpak et al. (2009) were 207.17 ± 5.800 and 29.13 ± 1.065 mm, respectively. The breadth of the proximal articular surface (BPC) was 19.40 ± 1.13 and 21.26 ± 1.27 in younger and older age groups, respectively; however, the same parameter was 18.1 ± 1.3 in Kibrya sheep (Demiraslan et al. 2018). The greatest length of tibia in older age groups was 198.20 ± 8.68, whereas it was 225.3 ± 3.2 mm in Kenguri sheep (Awate et al. 2020). The greatest breadth of the distal end and SB of the diaphysis of the tibia in Simien sheep was 24.7 ± 4 ± 0.99 and 10.15 ± 0.84 in younger groups and 23.35 ± 4.09 and 10.13 ± 0.82 mm in older groups, respectively (Table 3). However, the same parameter was found to be 25.8 ± 2.5 and 12.3 ± 2.1 mm in Kibyra sheep (Demiraslan et al. 2018) and 30.41 ± 2.457 and 16.92 ± 0.684 mm in adult Morkaraman sheep (Alpak et al. 2009), respectively.

The greatest length and smallest circumference of female Simien sheep metacarpals were 137.70 ± 7.75 and 11.70 ± 0.36 in younger groups, whereas 137.70 ± 7.75 and 12.06 ± 1.05 mm in older groups, respectively (Table 2); however, the measurements for Kibrya sheep by Demiraslan et al. (2018) were 121.5 ± 4.1 and 14.9 ± 1.5 mm; for indigenous France adult sheep by Guintard and Lallemand (2003), they were 18.4 ± 1.4 and 16.1 ± 2.3 mm, respectively. The greatest length, breadth of proximal end, breadth of distal end and SB of the diaphysis of metatarsal in Simien sheep were 138.87 ± 5.74, 18.88 ± 0.88, 21.46 ± 0.46 and 10.04 ± 0.20 mm in younger groups and 146.40 ± 8.24, 20.09 ± 1.05, 22.28 ± 1.37 and 10.40 ± 0.63 mm, respectively. However, the same measurement parameters were found to be 122.72 ± 7.80, 23.29 ± 1.31, 25.12 ± 1.04 and 13.55 ± 0.94 mm in adult Upper Anzaf Castle sheep bred by Onar et al. (2008) and 11.7 ± 1.2, 11.5 ± 1.2, 15.0 ± 1.3 and 13.5 ± 1.7 in unknown age sheep in France by Guintard and Lallemand (2003).

Insofar as it can be said the sheep breeds compared above differed in terms of the measurement parameters performed. The skeletal osteometric variations in size and shape are observed. The source of this variation in this study may be due to the genotypic diversity, age, nutrition, ecology and different growth rates and maturity patterns. In addition, sexual dimorphism might affect osteometrical values.

Comparisons of measurement variables were conducted, and most of the measurement variables were significantly different; this might be due to the study animals, which were different age groups with different growth patterns. But the insignificant differences in measurement values of the scapula (LG, BG), humerus (SD), ulna (SD), metacarpal (Bd, SD, CD), os coxae (SC, GBA), femur (SD, CD), tibia (Bd, SD) and metatarsal (GL, Bd, SD, CD). Furthermore, the measurement values of metapodials (metacarpal and metatarsal) were somewhat different in almost all measurement parameters. The magnitude of the differences in the means was insignificant except for metacarpal‐GL and metatarsal‐Bp. As Davis (1996) stated, the insignificant differences may characterize those parts of the skeleton which are not directly involved in joints. In addition, bone growth may cease before fusion, and the rates of growth in all bones are not equal both in size and length.

The majority of the coefficients of variation of the measurements (Tables 2 and 3) lie between 3 and 6. As Yablokov (1974) stated, the linear measurements of the post‐cranial skeleton in mammals generally lie between 3 and 5. In this sense, the variations were low between measurements in the two age groups, even though some larger variations were recorded. The high values of CVs may characterize those parts of the skeleton which are not directly involved in a joint or growth may cease before fusion. The rather low value of CV is important as far as it indicates no effect of parameters associated with the sampling of bones and thus allows the conclusion that they originate from a homogenous sample of the same district. In addition, a small part of this variation may also be due to observer error, as they are poorly defined measurements. Because the sheep skeletons studied here are all of the same sex and from the same district, any remaining variation may be due to variability in the animals’ health and inherited or residual individual variability.

sec.title: Osteo‐Morphological Investigations
sec.p: The scapula of the Simien sheep was flat and triangular, like that of the Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008) and blackbuck (Choudhary 2015), in that it is comparatively wider at the dorsal end and narrower at the ventral end. The scapula presented two surfaces and three borders. The medial surface presented a deeper sub‐scapular fossa, as reported by Siddiqui et al. (2008) in Black Bengal goat and Choudhary and Ishwer (2016) in blackbucks.

A prominent scapular spine divided the lateral surface into a smaller supra‐spinous and a much larger infra‐spinous fossa. Similar observations were noticed in goats and cattle (Getty et al. 1975). The two fossae are almost equal in dogs, as observed (Evans and Christensen 1979). The scapular spine was extended up to the neck in an almost straight line, and the free edge was prominent as in the Black Bengal goat (Siddiqui et al. 2008), and the study on the Bakarwali goat showed that the spine increased in height along with thickening at the middle (Sarma et al. 2003). Blagojević et al. (2016) also reported that the scapular spine is prominent in roe deer with acromion blade in the form of spike. A similar wavy pattern of the spine and well‐developed. The free edge of the spine did not subside by the acromion process and was less distinct in Simien sheep. In cattle and dogs, where the free edge of the spine did not subside but formed a prominent acromion process (Trotter and Lumb 1958; Getty et al. 1975) and was prominent in dogs also (Evans and Christensen 1979). Contrarily, the acromian process is prominent and plate‐like in Indian blackbuck (Choudhary and Singh 2016).

The vertebral border of the scapula was straight in Simien sheep but slightly convex in cattle (Getty et al. 1975) and goats (Getty et al. 1975). The neck of the scapula was very short and demarcated in Simien sheep. The nutrient foramen in the scapula of Simien sheep was found at the neck of the scapula, which was similar to goats (Getty et al. 1975) and cattle (Trotter and Lumb 1958). Central tuberosity of the spine was less distinct in Simien sheep than in Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008). The angle created by the acromion process was almost a straight line in Simien sheep as in goats (Getty et al. 1975). The articular surface of the glenoid cavity was rounded and concave, which is circular in cattle (Trotter and Lumb 1958). The coracoid process was ill‐distinct in Simien sheep from Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008).

Observing the whole humerus bone, where a slight curvature both in craniocaudal and mediolateral views was appreciable. The proximal extremity of the humerus comprises a medially projected head and two tuberosities (medial and lateral). The lateral tuberosity was much larger than the medial tuberosity in Simien sheep, but in cattle, both divisions are well developed (Getty et al. 1975). The bicipital groove was present between lateral and medial tuberosities. The anterior parts of both the lateral and medial tuberosities curved over the bicipital groove. Similar observations were recorded by Getty et al. (1975) in goats and Choudhary et al. (2013) in chital. On the contrary, the head is round and distinguishable from the lateral tuberosity in goats (Zedda et al. 2017), cattle (Getty et al. 1975) and oval in blackbuck (Choudhary 2015).

The shaft was slightly twisted and cylindrical. Similar observations are reported in goats (Makungu and Merere 2017). The lateral surface was spiral, smooth and presented a musculospiral groove. This was in agreement with the reports of Getty et al. (1975) in cattle and sheep and Siddiqui et al. (2008) in Black Bengal goats. The deltoid tuberosity of the Simien sheep was found less prominent in cattle (Getty et al. 1975) and Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008). The nutrient foramen was noticed more proximally located near the middle of the caudal surface. Siddiqui et al. (2008) also reported in Black Bengal goats.

The distal extremity consisted of two condyles, ill‐distinct epicondyles and radial and olecranon fossae. The medial condyle was much larger than the lateral condyle. The lateral aspect of the lateral condyle presented a distinct fossa which was not seen over the medial condyle. Similar observations are observed in cattle (Rajani et al. 2013) and goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008). The radial fossa was shallow, and the olecranon fossa was deeper. The cranial surface of the condyle of the humerus presented a shallow olecranon fossa and a concave radial fossa elongated mediolaterally sloped from proximomedial to distolateral, which is in agreement with a report described in Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008). Deep and wide radial and olecranon fossae often communicate with each other through a large supratrochlear foramen in dogs (Getty et al. 1975) and are ovoid, deep and almost vertically oriented in small East African goat (Makungu and Merere 2017). The articular surface of the condyle of the humerus presented medially located and grooved trochlea and laterally located capitulum. The trochlea was larger than the capitulum and extended more proximal than the capitulum. A similar observation was reported in small East African goats (Makungu and Merere 2017).

The radius was faintly curved and presented a shaft and two ends. The long shaft was flattened craniocaudally and curved longitudinally. The caudal surface was concave, and throughout the whole length along the lateral part, it was attached with the cranial surface of the shaft of the ulna except at the two interosseous spaces. Similar observations were noted in Black Bengal goat (Siddiqui et al. 2008), cattle and other sheep breeds (Getty et al. 1975). The body of the radius had a convex cranial surface and a concave caudal surface. It was larger and stout than the ulna. In the middle of the upper part of the anterior surface, there was a rough elevation, known as radial tuberosity; it was represented by a rough area just distal to the head of the radius on the craniomedial surface of the body. It is well‐profiled in cattle and better developed in other sheep than in goats (Getty et al. 1975). The medial styloid process of the radius was wide and convex. A similar observation was reported in small East African goats (Makungu and Merere 2017) and cattle (Getty et al. 1975).

The thin curved shaft and the distal extremity of the ulna were fused to a limited narrow area on the caudal surface of the radius. The two bones presented two interosseous spaces. Similar observations were reported in small East African goats (Makungu and Merere 2017), Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008), chital (Choudhary et al. 2013) and other sheep breeds (Getty et al. 1975). The distal interosseous space was small and indistinct. The proximal end was expanded and comprised one large olecranon process and a trochlear notch and articulates with the condyles of the humerus, whereas the distal extremity was placed laterally to the radius. At the anterior border of the trochlear notch, there was a beak‐like projection, and the anconeal process formed. The caudally directed olecranon process of the ulna was elongated and projected proximally beyond the radius. The olecranon tuber was elliptical but ovoid in small East African goats (Makungu and Merere 2017). The cranial rim of the proximal articular surface presents an ill‐distinct projection, known as the coronoid process. The distal end is articulated with the proximal row of carpal bones and presents three articular surfaces. The lateral styloid process of the ulna at the distal extremity was convex, pointed and extended below the radius.

In Simien sheep, the metacarpus consisted of a fused third and fourth metacarpal bone, which is only the large metacarpal bone, which was observed. Conversely, both the large and small metacarpal bones are observed in Black Bengal goats (Siddiqui et al. 2008) in the form of a thin cylinder with a pointed distal end. The dorsal and palmar surfaces had a dorsal and palmar longitudinal groove, respectively, near the midline, whereas in goats and cattle (Getty et al. 1975; Siddiqui et al. 2008), the groove is only faintly marked in the proximal and distal third of the shaft. The body of the fused third and fourth metacarpals was convex and flattened dorsal and palmar surfaces, respectively. Conversely, it was transversely flattened in its distal half and the proximal part; only slight concavity was observed in other species (Getty et al. 1975). The two concave articular areas of the proximal extremity were presented in Simien sheep. These prominent ridges are also described in Indian blackbuck (Choudhary 2015; Choudhary and Ishwer Singh 2016; Choudhary and Singh 2016) and goats (Getty et al. 1975). The distal end was marked by two condyles (lateral and medial) separated by an inter‐condyloid cleft, and facets for sesamoid bone were presented.

The pelvic girdle (os coxae) was a flat, irregular bone in Simien sheep. It consisted of two halves which were joined at the pelvic symphysis in the midline. Each part in turn consisted of three parts: the ilium, the ischium and the pubis, as reported in other ruminants (Frandson and Spurgeon 1992). Ilium was the largest of the three divisions and was irregularly triangular. The bone was flat and expanded above, narrow in the middle and slightly expanded below. The cranial border of the ilium bone was thick (known as an iliac crest), rough at the cranial ends and thin at the caudal end. The wing of the ilium formed the sacral and coxal tubers at its medial and lateral angles, respectively. The ischium was placed behind the ilium and the pubis. The cranial border forms the caudal margin of the obturator foramen. Its craniolateral angle formed the caudal part of the acetabulum, and its caudolateral angle formed the ischial tuber, whereas the caudomedial aspect joined its opposite to form ischiatic.

The pubis was the smallest bone of the os coxae and was placed between the ilium and ischium. The medial border of the ischium meets with the opposite part and forms the ischial symphysis. The medial border of the pubis is joined with the same border of the opposite bone at the pubic symphysis. The posterior border of the pubis was concave and formed the anterior boundary of the obturator foramen. Both the ischial symphysis and pubic symphysis were not ossified. Similar observations are reported in blackbuck (Choudhary 2015), cattle (Raghavan 1964), dogs (Miller et al. 1964) and other ruminants (Frandson and Spurgeon 1992).

The acetabulum was formed by the union of ilium, ischium and pubis as in cattle (Raghavan 1964) and in carnivores (König and Bragulla 2007). It was elliptical in shape, like blackbuck (Choudhary 2015) and cattle (Getty et al. 1975), but rounded and deep in alpaca (de Oliveira et al. 2007). On the ventral surface of the body cranial to the acetabulum, supra‐acetabular fossa was presented. The obturator foramen was a large oval opening found on the floor of the pelvis and was formed by the ischium and the pubis as in blackbuck (Choudhary 2015), cattle (Raghavan 1964) and dogs (Miller et al. 1964).

Different morphological details were observed in the Simien sheep femur, especially about the proximal and distal epiphyses and the shaft. It appears straighter, where a slight curvature in cranial, caudo‐medial and caudal views was appreciable. It was the second largest and the most massive long bone next to the tibia in Simien sheep, but dissimilar to the finding in cattle (Raghavan 1964) and in dogs (Miller et al. 1964).

The proximal extremity of the femur consisted of the head and neck medially and the greater trochanter laterally. The head showed an elliptical shape gradually passing into the trochanteric fossa. In contrast, the head is round and distinguishable from the trochanteric fossa in a goat (Zedda et al. 2017). There was a small, well‐defined circular‐shaped sulcus (fovea capitis) at the centre of the head as in cattle (Raghavan 1964) and in other ruminants (Akers and Denbow 2013). It was in disagreement with a report on dogs (Miller et al. 1964); an elongated notch and in goats (Zedda et al. 2017); frequently oval‐shaped. The greater trochanter was placed laterally and was very massive; its lateral surface was convex and rough, and the medial surface was concave as reported in cattle (Raghavan 1964) and goats (Zedda et al. 2017). The shaft was nearly cylindrical, as in blackbuck (Choudhary 2015). A small nutrient foramen was present in the proximal part of the cranial surface. Similar observations are reported in goats, but a small synovial dimple is present additionally (Zedda et al. 2017), which is absent in all the examined Simien sheep.

The distal extremity was large and composed of a trochlea in front and two condyles behind. The trochlea was composed of two ridges of lips separated by a groove. The medial and lateral ridges did not have a prominent size difference. Conversely, the medial ridge was much larger, more prominent and extended higher up than the lateral in cattle (Raghavan 1964) and other ruminants (Frandson and Spurgeon 1992). The condyles were medial and lateral, they were separated by a deep inter‐condyloid fossa, as reported in cattle (Raghavan 1964) and dogs (Miller et al. 1964). The lateral femoral condyle was larger than the medial femoral condyle. This is in disagreement with blackbuck, where the medial condyle was larger than the lateral condyle (Choudhary 2015). The supracondyloid fossa was visible on the distal part of the femur but located in the distal third of the caudolateral surface in goats (Getty et al. 1975) and blackbuck (Choudhary 2015). The curved trochanteric crest connected the greater trochanter obliquely to the lesser trochanter while creating the trochanteric fossa. The distal extremity presented caudally; the lateral condyle and the obliquely directed medial condyle are separated by the intercondylar fossa.

Tibia is the longest and heaviest bone in Simien sheep. Conversely, the tibia is about the same length as the femur in dogs (Abdulrahman and Abubakar Sadiq 2021). It presented a shaft and two extremities (proximal and distal). The shaft is slightly cylindrical, except near the extremities, where it is wider and compressed backwards; conversely, the tibia is regularly cylindrical, strongly curved in its lower two‐thirds and convex in front in the dog (Abdulrahman and Abubakar Sadiq 2021), distinctly curved in goat (Getty et al. 1975) and blackbuck (Choudhary 2015).

The proximal extremity consisted of the lateral and medial condyles separated by an intertuberal fossa, two articular eminences (facets for lateral and medial condyles), tibial spine and tibial tuberosities. The tibial tuberosities were separated by intertuberal fossa as in cattle (Raghavan 1964), dogs (König and Bragulla 2007) and other ruminants (Akers and Denbow 2013). The tibial spine was a central articular eminence divided into medial and lateral parts as in cattle (Raghavan 1964).

The distal extremity was much smaller than the proximal. There was presented an articular surface marked with depressions for articulation with the tibia tarsal bone. The fibula was absent, whereas the lateral malleolus was formed by a small irregular malleoli bone. But the fibula extends the entire length of the tibia in dogs (Abdulrahman and Abubakar Sadiq 2021), is fused with the lateral condyle of the tibia and continues with a small bone distally in goats (Getty et al. 1975), is partially fused with tibia at the tips in alpacas (de Oliveira et al. 2007), is rudimentary, and the head was fused with the lateral condyle of the tibia in blackbuck (Choudhary 2015).

The metatarsal bone had a shaft and two extremities (proximal and distal). The proximal extremity presented articular surfaces for tarsal articulation, and its dorsal surface was smooth. At the proximal end, it presents a metatarsal tuberosity, a small concave facet behind the metatarsal tuberosity and lateral articular areas as in blackbuck (Choudhary 2015) and cattle (Raghavan 1964). The distal extremity comprised lateral and medial condyle separated by an intercondyloid cleft, facets for sesamoid bone and axial articular area. The divided condyles were not straight, as in ox (Raghavan 1964) and blackbuck (Choudhary 2015). A shallow longitudinal groove was found at the proximal one‐third of the dorsal surface. The metatarsal canal was seen on the proximal and distal parts.

It is important to note that there were slight differences among members of the same family. This is consistent with the view of various investigators that interactions among genetics, adaptability and nutrition determine osteometric and osteo‐morphological characteristics (Richardson et al. 1976; Lawrence et al. 2012). Endogenous components are more relevant in this study. The animals’ nutritional state was not taken into account. The differences in measures derived from the limb bones of female Simien sheep were based on genetics and inherent growth factors.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study provides baseline osteometric and morphological data for female Simien sheep, supporting comparative studies with other indigenous or crossbred Ethiopian sheep. Despite a small sample size, significant differences were found in several measurement parameters related to age, whereas morphological characteristics were largely consistent, aside from size. The anatomical features align closely with those observed in other small ruminants and cattle. These findings contribute valuable reference information for academicians and comparative anatomists on relevant numerical and morphological information peculiar to this indigenous breed. Future research should explore larger sample sizes, advanced measurement techniques and the impacts of age and sexual dimorphism across species.

sec.title: Author Contributions
sec.p: Dagmawi Yitbarek: conceptualization, investigation, funding acquisition, writing – original draft, methodology, formal analysis, project administration, data curation, resources, writing – review and editing, software. Abebe Tesfaye Gessese: methodology, validation, visualization, writing – review and editing, supervision, investigation. Gashaw Getaneh Dagnaw: investigation, validation, visualization, writing – review and editing, data curation, supervision. Bimrew Admasu Mengistu: funding acquisition, writing – review and editing, validation, supervision, resources. Tewodros Fantahun: funding acquisition, validation, supervision, writing – review and editing, resources. Biruh Mebratu: funding acquisition, visualization, writing – review and editing. Mebrie Zemene Kinde: writing – review and editing, visualization, supervision. Yitayew Demessie: conceptualization, writing – review and editing, visualization, software, formal analysis, data curation.

sec.title: Ethics Statement
sec.p: The research involves animal experiments and has ethical issues. Promotion of high‐standard care and animal well‐being at all times has been done. So, before the beginning of the study, ethical clearance was approved by the Research and Ethics Committee of the College of Veterinary Medicine and Animal Sciences, University of Gondar, Gondar, Ethiopia (Decision No: CVMAS/13/905/2022).

sec.title: Conflicts of Interest
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: Peer Review
sec.p: The peer review history for this article is available at https://publons.com/publon/10.1002/vms3.70432.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: Authors acknowledge technicians at the University of Gondar's Veterinary Anatomy Laboratory for their assistance.

sec.title: Data Availability Statement
sec.p: Authors are ready to provide available data for a request via email and any communication platform.
